infobox.value: | [ILIYRICVM HODIERNVM, Quod Scriptores communiter SCLAVONIAM, Itali SCHIAVONIAM nuncupare solent, in Dalmatiam, Croatiam, Bosnam, et Slavoniam, from Atlas Van der Hagen](/wiki/File:Atlas_Van_der_Hagen-_IlIyricvm_Sclavoniam-_Bosnam_Dalmatiam_Slavoniam_Croatiam.jpg "ILIYRICVM HODIERNVM, Quod Scriptores communiter SCLAVONIAM, Itali SCHIAVONIAM nuncupare solent, in Dalmatiam, Croatiam, Bosnam, et Slavoniam, from Atlas Van der Hagen")

Slavonia is not designated as an official subdivision of [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"); it is a [historical region](/wiki/Regions_of_Croatia "Regions of Croatia").[[1]](#cite_note-Frucht_2004-1) The flag and arms below are also unofficial/historical; none are legally defined at present.  ---  **[^](#ref_box2)** The map represents modern-day perception: historical boundaries of Slavonia varied over centuries.  ---  **[^](#ref_box3)** The figures are an approximation based on statistical data for the five easternmost [Croatian counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia") ([Brod-Posavina](/wiki/Brod-Posavina_County "Brod-Posavina County"), [Osijek-Baranja](/wiki/Osijek-Baranja_County "Osijek-Baranja County"), [Požega-Slavonia](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Požega-Slavonia County"), [Virovitica-Podravina](/wiki/Virovitica-Podravina_County "Virovitica-Podravina County"), [Vukovar-Srijem](/wiki/Vukovar-Syrmia_County "Vukovar-Syrmia County")). | |

([/sləˈvoʊniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Slavonija*[[a]](#cite_note-2)) is one of the four [historical regions of Croatia](/wiki/Regions_of_Croatia "Regions of Croatia"), along with [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), [Croatia proper](/wiki/Croatia_proper "Croatia proper"), and [Istria](/wiki/Istria_County "Istria County").[[1]](#cite_note-Frucht_2004-1) Located in the [Pannonian Plain](/wiki/Pannonian_Plain "Pannonian Plain") and taking up the east of the country, it roughly corresponds with five [Croatian counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia"): [Brod-Posavina](/wiki/Brod-Posavina_County "Brod-Posavina County"), [Osijek-Baranja](/wiki/Osijek-Baranja_County "Osijek-Baranja County"), [Požega-Slavonia](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Požega-Slavonia County"), [Virovitica-Podravina](/wiki/Virovitica-Podravina_County "Virovitica-Podravina County"), and [Vukovar-Syrmia](/wiki/Vukovar-Syrmia_County "Vukovar-Syrmia County"), although the territory of the counties includes [Baranya](/wiki/Baranya_(region) "Baranya (region)"), and the definition of the western extent of Slavonia as a region varies. The counties cover 12,556 square kilometres (4,848 square miles) or 22.2% of Croatia, inhabited by 806,192—18.8% of Croatia's population. The largest city in the region is [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"), followed by [Slavonski Brod](/wiki/Slavonski_Brod "Slavonski Brod") and [Vinkovci](/wiki/Vinkovci "Vinkovci").

**Slavonia** ([/sləˈvoʊniə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"): *Slavonija*[[a]](#cite_note-2)) is one of the four [historical regions of Croatia](/wiki/Regions_of_Croatia "Regions of Croatia"), along with [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), [Croatia proper](/wiki/Croatia_proper "Croatia proper"), and [Istria](/wiki/Istria_County "Istria County").[[1]](#cite_note-Frucht_2004-1) Located in the [Pannonian Plain](/wiki/Pannonian_Plain "Pannonian Plain") and taking up the east of the country, it roughly corresponds with five [Croatian counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia"): [Brod-Posavina](/wiki/Brod-Posavina_County "Brod-Posavina County"), [Osijek-Baranja](/wiki/Osijek-Baranja_County "Osijek-Baranja County"), [Požega-Slavonia](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Požega-Slavonia County"), [Virovitica-Podravina](/wiki/Virovitica-Podravina_County "Virovitica-Podravina County"), and [Vukovar-Syrmia](/wiki/Vukovar-Syrmia_County "Vukovar-Syrmia County"), although the territory of the counties includes [Baranya](/wiki/Baranya_(region) "Baranya (region)"), and the definition of the western extent of Slavonia as a region varies. The counties cover 12,556 square kilometres (4,848 square miles) or 22.2% of Croatia, inhabited by 806,192—18.8% of Croatia's population. The largest city in the region is [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"), followed by [Slavonski Brod](/wiki/Slavonski_Brod "Slavonski Brod") and [Vinkovci](/wiki/Vinkovci "Vinkovci").

Slavonia is located in the [Pannonian Basin](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin"), largely bordered by the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava"), and [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") rivers. In the west, the region consists of the Sava and Drava valleys and the mountains surrounding the [Požega Valley](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega_Valley "Požega Valley"), and [plains](/wiki/Plain "Plain") in the east. Slavonia enjoys a moderate [continental climate](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") with relatively low precipitation.

After the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire"), which ruled the area of modern-day Slavonia until the 5th century, [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths") and [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") controlled the area before the arrival of [Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars") and [Slavs](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs"). The [Slavs in Lower Pannonia](/wiki/Slavs_in_Lower_Pannonia "Slavs in Lower Pannonia") established a principality in the 7th century, which was later incorporated into the [Kingdom of Croatia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(medieval) "Kingdom of Croatia (medieval)"); after its decline, the kingdom was ruled through a [personal union with Hungary](/wiki/Croatia_in_personal_union_with_Hungary "Croatia in personal union with Hungary"). In the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), the [Ban of Slavonia](/wiki/Ban_of_Slavonia "Ban of Slavonia") was the King's governor of these lands, at various times distinct from the [Ban of Croatia](/wiki/Ban_of_Croatia "Ban of Croatia").

The [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") conquest of Slavonia took place in the 16th century. At the turn of the 18th century, after the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") of 1683–1699, the [Treaty of Karlowitz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Karlowitz "Treaty of Karlowitz") transferred [Kingdom of Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Slavonia "Kingdom of Slavonia") to the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). After the [Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867"), Slavonia became part of the [Hungarian part of the realm](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Crown_of_Saint_Stephen "Lands of the Crown of Saint Stephen"), and a year later it became part of the [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia"). In 1918, when [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") dissolved, Slavonia became a part of the short-lived [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs") which in turn became a part of the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"), later renamed [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"). During the [Croatian War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence") of 1991–1995, Slavonia saw fierce fighting, including the 1991 [Battle of Vukovar](/wiki/Battle_of_Vukovar "Battle of Vukovar").

The economy of Slavonia is largely based on [processing industry](/wiki/Processing_industry "Processing industry"), trade, transport, and civil engineering. Agriculture is a significant component of its economy: Slavonia contains 45% of Croatia's agricultural land and accounts for a significant proportion of Croatia's livestock farming and production of [permanent crops](/wiki/Permanent_crop "Permanent crop"). The gross domestic product (GDP) of the five counties of Slavonia is worth 6,454 million [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") or 8,005 euro per capita, 27.5% below national average. The GDP of the five counties represents 13.6% of Croatia's GDP.

The cultural heritage of Slavonia represents a blend of historical influences, especially those from the end of the 17th century, when Slavonia started recovering from the [Ottoman wars](/wiki/Ottoman_wars_in_Europe "Ottoman wars in Europe"), and its traditional culture. Slavonia contributed to the culture of Croatia through art, writers, poets, sculptors, and [art patronage](/wiki/Patronage "Patronage"). In traditional music, Slavonia comprises a distinct region of Croatia, and the traditional culture is preserved through [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") festivals, with prominence given to [tamburica](/wiki/Tamburica "Tamburica") music and [bećarac](/wiki/Be%C4%87arac "Bećarac"), a form of traditional song, recognized as an [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"). The cuisine of Slavonia reflects diverse influences—a blend of traditional and foreign elements. Slavonia is one of Croatia's winemaking areas, with [Erdut](/wiki/Erdut "Erdut"), [Ilok](/wiki/Ilok "Ilok") and [Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo") recognized as centres of wine production.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of Croatia](/wiki/History_of_Croatia "History of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/ZGvucdove.jpg/250px-ZGvucdove.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZGvucdove.jpg)

[Vučedol Dove](/wiki/Vu%C4%8Dedol_Dove "Vučedol Dove")

The name *Slavonia* originated in the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"). The area was named after the [Slavs](/wiki/Slav "Slav") who settled there and called themselves \*Slověne. The root \*Slověn- appeared in various dialects of [languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages") spoken by people inhabiting the area west of the [Sutla](/wiki/Sutla "Sutla") river, as well as between the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") and [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava") rivers—[South Slavs](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") living in the area of the former [Illyricum](/wiki/Illyricum_(Roman_province) "Illyricum (Roman province)"). The area bounded by those rivers was called \*Slověnьje in the [Proto-Slavic](/wiki/Proto-Slavic "Proto-Slavic") language. The word subsequently evolved to its various present forms in the [Slavic languages](/wiki/Slavic_languages "Slavic languages"), and other languages adopted the term.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

### Prehistory and antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistory and antiquity")]

See also: [Prehistoric Croatia](/wiki/Prehistoric_Croatia "Prehistoric Croatia"), [Illyria](/wiki/Illyria "Illyria"), [Illyricum (Roman province)](/wiki/Illyricum_(Roman_province) "Illyricum (Roman province)"), and [Pannonia (Roman province)](/wiki/Pannonia_(Roman_province) "Pannonia (Roman province)")

Remnants of several [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") and [Chalcolithic](/wiki/Chalcolithic "Chalcolithic") cultures were found in all regions of Croatia,[[3]](#cite_note-4) but most of the sites are found in the river valleys of northern Croatia, including Slavonia. The most significant cultures whose presence was found include the [Starčevo culture](/wiki/Star%C4%8Devo_culture "Starčevo culture") whose finds were discovered near [Slavonski Brod](/wiki/Slavonski_Brod "Slavonski Brod") and [dated](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") to 6100–5200 [BC](/wiki/Anno_Domini "Anno Domini"),[[4]](#cite_note-5) the [Vučedol culture](/wiki/Vu%C4%8Dedol_culture "Vučedol culture"), the [Baden culture](/wiki/Baden_culture "Baden culture") and the [Kostolac culture](/wiki/Kostolac_culture "Kostolac culture").[[5]](#cite_note-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) Most finds attributed to the Baden and Vučedol cultures are discovered in the area near the right bank of the Danube near [Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar "Vukovar"), [Vinkovci](/wiki/Vinkovci "Vinkovci") and [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek"). The Baden culture sites in Slavonia are dated to 3600–3300 BC,[[7]](#cite_note-Heritage-Baden-8) and Vučedol culture finds are dated to 3000–2500 BC.[[8]](#cite_note-Heritage-Vučedol-9) The [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") left traces of the early [Illyrian](/wiki/Illyrians "Illyrians") [Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture") and the [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") [La Tène culture](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture").[[9]](#cite_note-10) Much later, the region was settled by Illyrians and other tribes, including the [Pannonians](/wiki/Pannonii "Pannonii"), who controlled much of present-day Slavonia. Even though archaeological finds of Illyrian settlements are much sparser than in areas closer to the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"), significant discoveries, for instance in [Kaptol](/wiki/Kaptol,_Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Kaptol, Požega-Slavonia County") near [Požega](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega,_Croatia "Požega, Croatia") have been made.[[10]](#cite_note-11) The Pannonians first came into contact with the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") in 35 BC, when the Romans conquered Segestica, or modern-day [Sisak](/wiki/Sisak "Sisak"). The conquest was completed in 11 BC, when the [Roman province of Illyricum](/wiki/Illyricum_(Roman_province) "Illyricum (Roman province)") was established, encompassing modern-day Slavonia as well as a vast territory on the right bank of Danube. The province was renamed Pannonia and divided within two decades.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

See also: [Pannonian Slavs § Principality](/wiki/Pannonian_Slavs#Principality "Pannonian Slavs"), [Kingdom of Croatia (medieval)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia_(medieval) "Kingdom of Croatia (medieval)"), and [Croatia in personal union with Hungary](/wiki/Croatia_in_personal_union_with_Hungary "Croatia in personal union with Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Medieval_pozega.jpg/250px-Medieval_pozega.jpg)](/wiki/File:Medieval_pozega.jpg)

Medieval [Požega](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega,_Croatia "Požega, Croatia")

After the collapse of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire"), which included the territory occupied by modern-day Slavonia, the area became a part of the [Ostrogothic Kingdom](/wiki/Ostrogothic_Kingdom "Ostrogothic Kingdom") by the end of the 5th century. However, control of the area proved a significant task, and [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") were given increasing control of Pannonia in the 6th century, which ended in their withdrawal in 568 and the arrival of [Pannonian Avars](/wiki/Pannonian_Avars "Pannonian Avars") and Slavs, who established control of Pannonia by the year 582.[[12]](#cite_note-13) After the fall of the Avar Khaganate at the beginning of the 9th century, in [Lower Pannonia there was a principality](/wiki/Pannonian_Slavs#Principality "Pannonian Slavs"), governed by Slavic rulers who were vassals of [Francs](/wiki/Francia "Francia"). The [invasion of the Hungarian tribes](/wiki/Hungarian_invasions_of_Europe "Hungarian invasions of Europe") overwhelmed this state. The eastern part of Slavonia in the 9th century may have been ruled by [Bulgars](/wiki/First_Bulgarian_Empire "First Bulgarian Empire").[[13]](#cite_note-14) The first king of Croatia [Tomislav](/wiki/Tomislav_I_of_Croatia "Tomislav I of Croatia") defeated Hungarian and [Bulgarian invasions](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Bulgarian_battle_of_926 "Croatian–Bulgarian battle of 926") and spread the influence of Croatian kings northward to Slavonia.[[14]](#cite_note-Posavec-15) The medieval Croatian kingdom reached its peak in the 11th century during the reigns of [Petar Krešimir IV](/wiki/Petar_Kre%C5%A1imir_IV_of_Croatia "Petar Krešimir IV of Croatia") (1058–1074) and [Dmitar Zvonimir](/wiki/Demetrius_Zvonimir_of_Croatia "Demetrius Zvonimir of Croatia") (1075–1089).[[15]](#cite_note-Margetić-16) When [Stjepan II](/wiki/Stephen_II_of_Croatia "Stephen II of Croatia") died in 1091, ending the [Trpimirović](/wiki/House_of_Trpimirovi%C4%87 "House of Trpimirović") dynasty, [Ladislaus I of Hungary](/wiki/Ladislaus_I_of_Hungary "Ladislaus I of Hungary") claimed the Croatian crown. Opposition to the claim led to a [war](/wiki/Battle_of_Gvozd_Mountain "Battle of Gvozd Mountain") and [personal union of Croatia and Hungary](/wiki/Croatia_in_personal_union_with_Hungary "Croatia in personal union with Hungary") in 1102, ruled by [Coloman](/wiki/Coloman_of_Hungary "Coloman of Hungary").[[16]](#cite_note-HR-HU-Heka-17) In the 2nd half of the 12th century, Croatia and the territory between the Drava and the Sava were governed by the [ban of all Slavonia](/wiki/Ban_of_Slavonia "Ban of Slavonia"), appointed by the king. From the 13th century, a separate ban governed parts of present-day [central Croatia](/wiki/Central_Croatia "Central Croatia"), western Slavonia, and northwestern [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), an area where a new entity emerged named Kingdom of Slavonia ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *regnum Sclavoniae*), while modern-day eastern Slavonia was a part of Hungary. Croatia and Slavonia were in 1476 united under the same [ban](/wiki/Ban_of_Croatia "Ban of Croatia") ([viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy")), but kept separate parliaments until 1558.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Croatia_15th_century_map.png/250px-Croatia_15th_century_map.png)](/wiki/File:Croatia_15th_century_map.png)

Map of Slavonia in the 15th century

The [Ottoman conquests in Croatia](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_War "Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War") led to the 1493 [Battle of Krbava field](/wiki/Battle_of_Krbava_field "Battle of Krbava field") and 1526 [Battle of Mohács](/wiki/Battle_of_Moh%C3%A1cs "Battle of Mohács"), both ending in decisive Ottoman victories. King [Louis II of Hungary](/wiki/Louis_II_of_Hungary "Louis II of Hungary") died at Mohács, and [Ferdinand I](/wiki/Ferdinand_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Ferdinand I, Holy Roman Emperor") of the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") was [elected in 1527](/wiki/1527_election_in_Cetin "1527 election in Cetin") as the new ruler of Croatia, under the condition that he provide protection to Croatia against the Ottoman Empire, while respecting its political rights.[[18]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-19)[[19]](#cite_note-frucht422-20) The period saw the rise to prominence of a native nobility such as the [Frankopans](/wiki/Frankopan "Frankopan") and the [Šubićs](/wiki/%C5%A0ubi%C4%87 "Šubić"), and ultimately to numerous [bans](/wiki/Ban_(title) "Ban (title)") from the two families.[[20]](#cite_note-Font-21) The present coat of arms of Slavonia, used in an official capacity as a part of the [coat of arms of Croatia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Croatia "Coat of arms of Croatia"),[[21]](#cite_note-Arms-Act-22) dates from this period—it was granted to Slavonia by king [Vladislaus II Jagiellon](/wiki/Vladislaus_II_of_Bohemia_and_Hungary "Vladislaus II of Bohemia and Hungary") on 8 December 1496.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

### Ottoman conquest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ottoman conquest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Luka_Ibrisimovic-Sokol.jpg/250px-Luka_Ibrisimovic-Sokol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luka_Ibrisimovic-Sokol.jpg)

[Luka Ibrišimović](/wiki/Luka_Ibri%C5%A1imovi%C4%87 "Luka Ibrišimović") led a revolt against Ottomans in Požega.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

Main articles: [Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_War "Hundred Years' Croatian–Ottoman War"), [Sanjak of Pojega](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Pojega "Sanjak of Pojega"), and [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War")

Following the Battle of Mohács, the Ottomans expanded their possessions in Slavonia seizing [Đakovo](/wiki/%C4%90akovo "Đakovo") in 1536 and Požega in 1537, defeating a Habsburg army led by [Johann Katzianer](/wiki/Johann_Katzianer "Johann Katzianer"), who was attempting to retake Slavonia, at [Gorjani](/wiki/Gorjani,_Croatia "Gorjani, Croatia") in September 1537. By 1540, Osijek was also under firm control of the Ottomans, and regular administration in Slavonia was introduced by establishing the [Sanjak of Pojega](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Pojega "Sanjak of Pojega"). The Ottoman control in Slavonia expanded as [Novska](/wiki/Novska "Novska") surrendered the same year. Turkish conquest continued—[Našice](/wiki/Na%C5%A1ice "Našice") were seized in 1541, [Orahovica](/wiki/Orahovica "Orahovica") and [Slatina](/wiki/Slatina,_Croatia "Slatina, Croatia") in 1542, and in 1543, [Voćin](/wiki/Vo%C4%87in "Voćin"), [Sirač](/wiki/Sira%C4%8D "Sirač") and, after a 40-day siege, [Valpovo](/wiki/Valpovo "Valpovo"). In 1544, Ottoman forces conquered [Pakrac](/wiki/Pakrac "Pakrac"). Lessening hostilities brought about a five-year truce in 1547 and temporary stabilization of the border between Habsburg and Ottoman empires, with [Virovitica](/wiki/Virovitica "Virovitica") becoming the most significant defensive Habsburg fortress and Požega the most significant Ottoman centre in Slavonia, as Ottoman advances to Sisak and [Čazma](/wiki/%C4%8Cazma "Čazma") were made, including a brief occupation of the cities. Further westward efforts of the [Turkish forces](/wiki/Military_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Military of the Ottoman Empire") presented a significant threat to [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb") and the rest of Croatia and the Hungarian kingdom, prompting a greater defensive commitment by the [Habsburg Monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"). One year after the 1547 truce ended, [Ivan Lenković](/wiki/Ivan_Lenkovi%C4%87 "Ivan Lenković") devised a system of fortifications and troops in the border areas, a forerunner of the [Croatian Military Frontier](/wiki/Croatian_Military_Frontier "Croatian Military Frontier"). Nonetheless, in 1552, the Ottoman conquest of Slavonia was completed when Virovitica was captured.[[24]](#cite_note-Mujadžević-25) Ottoman advances in the Croatian territory continued until the 1593 [Battle of Sisak](/wiki/Battle_of_Sisak "Battle of Sisak"), the first decisive Ottoman defeat, and a more lasting stabilisation of the frontier. During the [Great Turkish War](/wiki/Great_Turkish_War "Great Turkish War") (1683–1698), Slavonia was regained in between 1684 and 1691 when the Ottomans abandoned the region—unlike western [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia "Bosnia"), which had been part of Croatia before the [Ottoman conquest](/wiki/Ottoman_conquest_of_Bosnia "Ottoman conquest of Bosnia").[[19]](#cite_note-frucht422-20) The present-day southern border of Slavonia and the border between Croatia and [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina") is a remnant of this outcome.[[25]](#cite_note-Lane409-26)[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Martes_foina_2.jpg/250px-Martes_foina_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martes_foina_2.jpg)

The [marten](/wiki/Marten "Marten") is a heraldic symbol of Slavonia, representing the historical region's coat of arms. It depicts a marten running on a red field between two wavy white lines, all on a blue background with a star on top

The [Ottoman wars](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Ottoman_Wars "Croatian–Ottoman Wars") instigated great demographic changes. Croats migrated towards [Austria](/wiki/Archduchy_of_Austria "Archduchy of Austria") and the present-day [Burgenland Croats](/wiki/Burgenland_Croats "Burgenland Croats") are direct descendants of these settlers.[[27]](#cite_note-BurgenlandCro-28) The Muslim population in Slavonia at the end of Turkish rule accounted for almost half of Slavonia's population who was indigenous, primarily Croats, less immigrants from Bosnia and Serbia and rarely genuine Turks or Arabs.[[28]](#cite_note-29) In the second half of the 16th century Vlachs from Slavonia were no longer an exclusive part of population because the Vlach privileges were attractive for many non-Vlachs who mixed with the Vlachs in order to get their status.[[29]](#cite_note-30) To replace the fleeing Croats, the Habsburgs called on the [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") populations of [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia "Bosnia") and [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia") to provide military service in the Croatian Military Frontier. Serb migration into this region peaked during the [Great Serb Migrations](/wiki/Great_Serb_Migrations "Great Serb Migrations") of 1690 and 1737–39.[[30]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-31) The greatest Serb concentrations were in the eastern Slavonia, and [Sremski Karlovci](/wiki/Sremski_Karlovci "Sremski Karlovci") became the see of [Serbian Orthodox](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") metropolitans.[[31]](#cite_note-Banac2015-32) Part of the colonists came to Slavonia from area south of the [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava"), especially from the [Soli](/wiki/Tuzla "Tuzla") and [Usora](/wiki/Usora_Municipality "Usora Municipality") areas, continuing the process which already started after 1521. At beginning of the 17th century it seems that there was a new wave of colonization, about 10,000 families which are assumed to come from [Sanjak of Klis](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Klis "Sanjak of Klis") or with less possibility from area of [Sanjak of Bosnia](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Bosnia "Sanjak of Bosnia").[[32]](#cite_note-33)

### Habsburg Monarchy and Austria-Hungary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Habsburg Monarchy and Austria-Hungary")]

Main articles: [Kingdom of Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Slavonia "Kingdom of Slavonia"), [Triune Kingdom of Croatia](/wiki/Triune_Kingdom_of_Croatia "Triune Kingdom of Croatia"), [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia"), and [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Dvorac_Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87._Na%C5%A1ice.jpg/250px-Dvorac_Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87._Na%C5%A1ice.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dvorac_Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87._Na%C5%A1ice.jpg)

[Pejačević Castle](/wiki/Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87_Castle_in_Na%C5%A1ice "Pejačević Castle in Našice") of [Pejačević family](/wiki/House_of_Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87 "House of Pejačević") in [Našice](/wiki/Na%C5%A1ice "Našice")

The areas acquired through the [Treaty of Karlowitz](/wiki/Treaty_of_Karlowitz "Treaty of Karlowitz") were assigned to Croatia, itself in the union with Hungary and the union ruled by the Habsburgs. The border area along the [Una](/wiki/Una_(Sava) "Una (Sava)"), Sava and Danube rivers became the [Slavonian Military Frontier](/wiki/Slavonian_Military_Frontier "Slavonian Military Frontier"). At this time, Osijek took over the role of the administrative and military centre of the newly formed [Kingdom of Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Slavonia "Kingdom of Slavonia") from Požega.[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27) The 1830s and 1840s saw [romantic nationalism](/wiki/Romantic_nationalism "Romantic nationalism") inspire the [Croatian National Revival](/wiki/Illyrian_movement "Illyrian movement"), a political and cultural campaign advocating unity of all [South Slavs](/wiki/South_Slavs "South Slavs") in the empire. Its primary focus was the establishment of a standard language as a counterweight to [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"), along with the promotion of [Croatian literature](/wiki/Croatian_literature "Croatian literature") and [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Croatia "Culture of Croatia").[[33]](#cite_note-CRIS-Stančić-34) During the [Hungarian Revolution of 1848](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1848 "Hungarian Revolution of 1848") Croatia sided with the Austrians, Ban [Josip Jelačić](/wiki/Josip_Jela%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Josip Jelačić") helping to defeat the Hungarian forces in 1849, and ushering in a period of [Germanization](/wiki/Germanization "Germanization") policy.[[34]](#cite_note-Ante_Čuvalo_2008_13–27-35) By the 1860s, failure of the policy became apparent, leading to the [Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867") and creation of a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") between the crowns of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"). The treaty left the issue of Croatia's status to Hungary as a part of [Transleithania](/wiki/Transleithania "Transleithania")—and the status was resolved by the [Croatian–Hungarian Settlement](/wiki/Croatian%E2%80%93Hungarian_Settlement "Croatian–Hungarian Settlement") of 1868, when the kingdoms of Croatia and Slavonia were united as the [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia").[[35]](#cite_note-36) After [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") occupied [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Condominium_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Condominium of Bosnia and Herzegovina") following the [1878 Treaty of Berlin](/wiki/Treaty_of_Berlin_(1878) "Treaty of Berlin (1878)"), the Military Frontiers were abolished and the Croatian and Slavonian Military Frontier territory returned to Croatia-Slavonia in 1881,[[19]](#cite_note-frucht422-20) pursuant to provisions of the Croatian-Hungarian Settlement.[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-Dubravica-38) At that time, the easternmost point of Croatia-Slavonia became [Zemun](/wiki/Zemun "Zemun"), as all of [Syrmia](/wiki/Syrmia "Syrmia") was encompassed by the kingdom.[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27)

### Kingdom of Yugoslavia and World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kingdom of Yugoslavia and World War II")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/St._Peter%27s_Cathedral%2C_Dakovo.jpg/250px-St._Peter%27s_Cathedral%2C_Dakovo.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Peter%27s_Cathedral,_Dakovo.jpg)

[Cathedral of St. Peter in Đakovo, a distinctive symbol of Slavonia](/wiki/%C4%90akovo_Cathedral "Đakovo Cathedral")[[38]](#cite_note-39)

See also: [Creation of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Creation_of_Yugoslavia "Creation of Yugoslavia"), [Kingdom of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"), [Banovina of Croatia](/wiki/Banovina_of_Croatia "Banovina of Croatia"), [World War II in Yugoslavia](/wiki/World_War_II_in_Yugoslavia "World War II in Yugoslavia"), and [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia")

On 29 October 1918, the Croatian Sabor declared independence and decided to join the newly formed [State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs](/wiki/State_of_Slovenes,_Croats_and_Serbs "State of Slovenes, Croats and Serbs"),[[18]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-19) which in turn entered into union with the [Kingdom of Serbia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia "Kingdom of Serbia") on 4 December 1918 to form the [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbs,_Croats,_and_Slovenes "Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes").[[39]](#cite_note-40) The [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon") was signed in 1920, at the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), between the [Allies of World War I](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") and [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") as one of the successor states to Austria-Hungary.[[40]](#cite_note-Craig66-41) The treaty established the southern border of Hungary along the Drava and [Mura](/wiki/Mur_(river) "Mur (river)") rivers, except in [Baranya](/wiki/Baranya_(former_county) "Baranya (former county)"), where only the northern part of the county was kept by Hungary.[[41]](#cite_note-Columbia-42)[[42]](#cite_note-encyclopedia_of_ww1-43) The territorial acquisition in Baranya was not made a part of Slavonia, even though adjacent to Osijek, because pre-1918 administrative divisions were disestablished by the new kingdom.[[43]](#cite_note-44) The political situation in the new kingdom deteriorated, leading to the [dictatorship](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship") of King [Alexander](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Yugoslavia "Alexander I of Yugoslavia") in January 1929.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The dictatorship formally ended in 1931 when the king imposed a more unitarian constitution transferring executive power to the king, and changed the name of the country to Yugoslavia.[[45]](#cite_note-46) The [Cvetković–Maček Agreement](/wiki/Cvetkovi%C4%87%E2%80%93Ma%C4%8Dek_Agreement "Cvetković–Maček Agreement") of August 1939 created the autonomous [Banovina of Croatia](/wiki/Banovina_of_Croatia "Banovina of Croatia") incorporating Slavonia. Pursuant to the agreement, the Yugoslav government retained control of defence, internal security, foreign affairs, trade, and transport while other matters were left to the Croatian Sabor and a crown-appointed 'Ban'.[[46]](#cite_note-Klemencic-Zagar-121-123-47)

In April 1941, [Yugoslavia was occupied](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia") by [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy"). Following the invasion the territory of Slavonia was incorporated into the [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia"), a Nazi-backed [puppet state](/wiki/Puppet_state "Puppet state") and assigned as a zone under German occupation for the duration of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The regime introduced [anti-semitic](/wiki/Antisemitism "Antisemitism") laws and conducted a campaign of ethnic cleansing and [genocide against Serb](/wiki/Genocide_of_Serbs_in_the_Independent_State_of_Croatia "Genocide of Serbs in the Independent State of Croatia") and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Croatia "Romani people in Croatia") populations,[[47]](#cite_note-Klemencic-Zagar-153-156-48) exemplified by the [Jasenovac](/wiki/Jasenovac_concentration_camp "Jasenovac concentration camp") and [Stara Gradiška](/wiki/Stara_Gradi%C5%A1ka_concentration_camp "Stara Gradiška concentration camp") concentration camps,[[48]](#cite_note-49) but to a much lesser extent in Slavonia than in other regions, due to strategic interests of the Axis in keeping peace in the area.[[49]](#cite_note-Jelic-50) The largest [massacre](/wiki/Vo%C4%87in_massacre_(1942) "Voćin massacre (1942)") occurred in 1942 in [Voćin](/wiki/Vo%C4%87in "Voćin").[[50]](#cite_note-51)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Armed resistance soon developed in the region, and by 1942, the [Yugoslav Partisans](/wiki/Yugoslav_Partisans "Yugoslav Partisans") controlled substantial territories, especially in mountainous parts of Slavonia.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The Serbian royalist [Chetniks](/wiki/Chetniks "Chetniks"), who carried out [genocide against Croat](/wiki/Chetnik_war_crimes_in_World_War_II "Chetnik war crimes in World War II") civilian population,[[52]](#cite_note-Klemencic-Zagar-184-53) struggled to establish a significant presence in Slavonia throughout the war.[[49]](#cite_note-Jelic-50) Partisans led by [Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito") took full control of Slavonia in April 1945.[[53]](#cite_note-54) After the war, the new Yugoslav government interned local [Germans](/wiki/Germans_of_Croatia "Germans of Croatia") in camps in Slavonia, the largest of which were in [Valpovo](/wiki/Valpovo_work_camp "Valpovo work camp") and Krndija, where many died of hunger and diseases.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### Federal Yugoslavia and the independence of Croatia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Federal Yugoslavia and the independence of Croatia")]

See also: [Socialist Republic of Croatia](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia") and [Croatian War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/Mailath_Castle_in_Donji_Miholjac_%283%29.jpg/250px-Mailath_Castle_in_Donji_Miholjac_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mailath_Castle_in_Donji_Miholjac_(3).jpg)

Castle Mailáth, [Donji Miholjac](/wiki/Donji_Miholjac "Donji Miholjac").

After World War II, Croatia—including Slavonia—became a [single-party](/wiki/Single-party "Single-party") [Socialist federal unit](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Croatia "Socialist Republic of Croatia") of the [Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia"), ruled by the [Communists](/wiki/League_of_Communists_of_Croatia "League of Communists of Croatia"), but enjoying a degree of autonomy within the federation. The autonomy effectively increased after the [1974 Yugoslav Constitution](/wiki/1974_Yugoslav_Constitution "1974 Yugoslav Constitution"), basically fulfilling a goal of the [Croatian Spring](/wiki/Croatian_Spring "Croatian Spring") movement, and providing a legal basis for independence of the federative constituents.[[55]](#cite_note-Rich-56) In 1947, when all borders of the former Yugoslav constituent republics had been defined by demarcation commissions, pursuant to decisions of the [AVNOJ](/wiki/AVNOJ "AVNOJ") of 1943 and 1945, the federal organization of *Yugoslav Baranya* was defined as Croatian territory allowing its integration with Slavonia. The commissions also set up the present-day 317.6-kilometre (197.3 mi) border between Serbia and Croatia in Syrmia, and along the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") River between [Ilok](/wiki/Ilok "Ilok") and mouth of the Drava and further north to the Hungarian border, the section south of confluence of the Drava matching the border between the [Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Croatia-Slavonia "Kingdom of Croatia-Slavonia") and the [Bács-Bodrog County](/wiki/B%C3%A1cs-Bodrog_County "Bács-Bodrog County") that existed until 1918 and the end of World War I.[[56]](#cite_note-Kraljević-Razgraničenje-1947-57)

The [1964 Slavonia earthquake](/wiki/1964_Slavonia_earthquake "1964 Slavonia earthquake") caused widespread devastation and several human casualties. A large area of the region entered a period of several years of reconstruction afterwards.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJosipović_Batorek2013195-58)

In the 1980s the political situation in Yugoslavia deteriorated with national tension fanned by the 1986 Serbian [SANU Memorandum](/wiki/Memorandum_of_the_Serbian_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "Memorandum of the Serbian Academy of Sciences and Arts") and the [1989 coups in Vojvodina, Kosovo and Montenegro](/wiki/1989_coups_in_Vojvodina,_Kosovo_and_Montenegro "1989 coups in Vojvodina, Kosovo and Montenegro").[[58]](#cite_note-frucht433-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60) In January 1990, the [Communist Party](/wiki/League_of_Communists_of_Yugoslavia "League of Communists of Yugoslavia") fragmented along national lines, with the Croatian [faction](/wiki/Political_faction "Political faction") demanding a looser federation.[[60]](#cite_note-Pauković-14Congress-2009-61) In the same year, the [first multi-party elections](/wiki/Croatian_parliamentary_election,_1990 "Croatian parliamentary election, 1990") were held in Croatia, with [Franjo Tuđman](/wiki/Franjo_Tu%C4%91man "Franjo Tuđman")'s win raising nationalist tensions further.[[61]](#cite_note-Independent-Tuđman-Obituary-62) The [Serbs in Croatia](/wiki/Serbs_of_Croatia "Serbs of Croatia"), intent on achieving independence from Croatia, left the Sabor and declared the autonomy of areas that would soon become the unrecognized self-declared [Republic of Serbian Krajina](/wiki/Republic_of_Serbian_Krajina "Republic of Serbian Krajina") (RSK).[[62]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Autonomy-AUG1990-63)[[63]](#cite_note-EE-CIS-book-64) As tensions rose, Croatia [declared independence](/wiki/Croatian_independence_referendum,_1991 "Croatian independence referendum, 1991") in June 1991; however the declaration came into effect on 8 October 1991.[[64]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Declaration-26June1991-65)[[65]](#cite_note-Sabor-Independence-8Oct1991-66) Tensions escalated into the [Croatian War of Independence](/wiki/Croatian_War_of_Independence "Croatian War of Independence") when the [Yugoslav National Army](/wiki/Yugoslav_National_Army "Yugoslav National Army") and various Serb [paramilitaries](/wiki/Paramilitary "Paramilitary") attacked Croatia.[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Otkos10-67) By the end of 1991, a high intensity war fought along a wide front reduced Croatia to controlling about two-thirds of its territory.[[67]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Iceland-68)[[68]](#cite_note-LATimes-OccupiedPct-1Aug91-69)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery%2C_20150429160343.jpg/250px-Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery%2C_20150429160343.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vukovar_Memorial_Cemetery,_20150429160343.jpg)

[Vukovar](/wiki/Vukovar "Vukovar") memorial cemetery

In Slavonia, the first armed conflicts were clashes in [Pakrac](/wiki/Pakrac_clash "Pakrac clash"),[[69]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Pakrac-3Mar1991-70)[[70]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Pakrac-4Mar1991-71) and [Borovo Selo](/wiki/Borovo_Selo_killings "Borovo Selo killings") near Vukovar.[[71]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Borovo-5May1991-72)[[72]](#cite_note-WarInBalkans-CraigNation-105-73) [Western Slavonia was occupied](/wiki/SAO_Western_Slavonia "SAO Western Slavonia") in August 1991, following an advance by the [Yugoslav forces](/wiki/Yugoslav_People%27s_Army "Yugoslav People's Army") north from [Banja Luka](/wiki/Banja_Luka "Banja Luka") across the Sava River.[[73]](#cite_note-Bjelajac-Žunec-245-74) This was partially pushed back by the [Croatian Army](/wiki/Croatian_Army "Croatian Army") in operations named [Otkos 10](/wiki/Operation_Otkos_10 "Operation Otkos 10"),[[66]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Otkos10-67) and [Orkan 91](/wiki/Operation_Orkan_91 "Operation Orkan 91"), which established a front line around [Okučani](/wiki/Oku%C4%8Dani "Okučani") and south of Pakrac that would hold virtually unchanged for more than three years until [Operation Flash](/wiki/Operation_Flash "Operation Flash") in May 1995.[[74]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Flash-May2-75) Armed conflict in the [eastern Slavonia](/wiki/SAO_Eastern_Slavonia,_Baranja_and_Western_Syrmia "SAO Eastern Slavonia, Baranja and Western Syrmia"), culminating in the [Battle of Vukovar](/wiki/Battle_of_Vukovar "Battle of Vukovar") and a subsequent [massacre](/wiki/Vukovar_massacre "Vukovar massacre"),[[75]](#cite_note-NYT-Vukovar-Captured-76)[[76]](#cite_note-Independent-Vukovar-Massacre-77) also included heavy fighting and the successful defence of Osijek and Vinkovci. The front line stabilized and a ceasefire was agreed to on 2 January 1992, coming into force the next day.[[77]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Jan3-Ceasefire-78) After the ceasefire, [United Nations Protection Force](/wiki/United_Nations_Protection_Force "United Nations Protection Force") was deployed to the occupied areas,[[78]](#cite_note-UN-deployment-29Jan91-79) but intermittent artillery and rocket attacks, launched from [Serb-held areas of Bosnia](/wiki/Republika_Srpska "Republika Srpska"), continued in several areas of Slavonia, especially in Slavonski Brod and [Županja](/wiki/%C5%BDupanja "Županja").[[79]](#cite_note-SlavonskiBrod-Bombardment-80)[[80]](#cite_note-Hrvatsko_kulturno_vijeće-81) The war effectively ended in 1995 with Croatia achieving a [decisive victory](/wiki/Operation_Storm "Operation Storm") over the RSK in August 1995.[[81]](#cite_note-LATimes-Storm-Complete-82) The remaining occupied areas—eastern Slavonia—were restored to Croatia pursuant to the [Erdut Agreement](/wiki/Erdut_Agreement "Erdut Agreement") of November 1995, with the process concluded in mid-January 1998.[[82]](#cite_note-NYTimes-UNTAES-16Jan98-83)

After the war, a number of towns and municipalities in the region were designated [Areas of Special State Concern](/wiki/Areas_of_Special_State_Concern_(Croatia) "Areas of Special State Concern (Croatia)").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Political geography")]

The [Croatian counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Croatia "Counties of Croatia") were re-established in 1992, but their borders changed in some instances, with the latest revision taking place in 2006.[[83]](#cite_note-CountiesAct2006-84) Slavonia consists of five counties—[Brod-Posavina](/wiki/Brod-Posavina_County "Brod-Posavina County"), [Osijek-Baranja](/wiki/Osijek-Baranja_County "Osijek-Baranja County"), [Požega-Slavonia](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Požega-Slavonia County"), [Virovitica-Podravina](/wiki/Virovitica-Podravina_County "Virovitica-Podravina County") and [Vukovar-Syrmia](/wiki/Vukovar-Srijem_County "Vukovar-Srijem County") counties—which largely cover the territory historically associated with Slavonia. The western borders of the five-county territory lie in the area where the western boundary of Slavonia generally has been located since the Ottoman conquest, with the remaining borders being at the international borders of [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia").[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27) This places the Croatian part of [Baranya](/wiki/Baranya_(region) "Baranya (region)") into the Slavonian counties, constituting the Eastern Croatia [macroregion](/wiki/Macroregion "Macroregion").[[84]](#cite_note-Babić-85) Terms *Eastern Croatia* and *Slavonia* are increasingly used as synonyms.[[85]](#cite_note-86) The Brod-Posavina County comprises two [cities](/wiki/Cities_of_Croatia "Cities of Croatia")—Slavonski Brod and [Nova Gradiška](/wiki/Nova_Gradi%C5%A1ka "Nova Gradiška")—and 26 [Municipalities of Croatia](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Croatia "Municipalities of Croatia").[[86]](#cite_note-BPZ-87) The Osijek-Baranja County consists of seven cities—[Beli Manastir](/wiki/Beli_Manastir "Beli Manastir"), [Belišće](/wiki/Beli%C5%A1%C4%87e "Belišće"), [Donji Miholjac](/wiki/Donji_Miholjac "Donji Miholjac"), Đakovo, Našice, Osijek and Valpovo—and 35 municipalities.[[87]](#cite_note-88) The Požega-Slavonia County comprises five cities—[Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo"), [Lipik](/wiki/Lipik "Lipik"), Pakrac, [Pleternica](/wiki/Pleternica "Pleternica") and Požega—and five municipalities.[[88]](#cite_note-PSZ-89) The Virovitica-Podravina County covers three cities—Orahovica, Slatina and Virovitica—and 13 municipalities.[[89]](#cite_note-VPZ-90) The Vukovar-Srijem County encompasses five cities—Ilok, [Otok](/wiki/Otok,_Vukovar-Srijem_County "Otok, Vukovar-Srijem County"), Vinkovci, Vukovar and Županja—and 26 municipalities.[[90]](#cite_note-VSZ-91) The whole of Slavonia is the eastern half of Central and Eastern (Pannonian) Croatia [NUTS-2 statistical unit of Croatia](/wiki/NUTS_of_Croatia "NUTS of Croatia"), together with further areas of [Central Croatia](/wiki/Central_Croatia "Central Croatia"). Other statistical units correspond to the counties, cities and municipalities.[[91]](#cite_note-92) The five counties combined cover area size of 12,556 square kilometres (4,848 square miles), representing 22.2% of territory of Croatia.[[92]](#cite_note-DZS-Stat2010-93)

### Physical geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Physical geography")]

See also: [Geography of Croatia](/wiki/Geography_of_Croatia "Geography of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Sava_River_Aerial.jpg/250px-Sava_River_Aerial.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sava_River_Aerial.jpg)

The [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") River forms a natural border between the left Slavonian [Sava](/wiki/Sava "Sava") bank in Croatia and the right bank of the Sava River in Bosnia and Herzegovina.

The boundaries of Slavonia, as a geographical region, do not necessarily coincide with the borders of the five counties, except in the south and east where the Sava and Danube rivers define them. The international borders of Croatia are boundaries common to both definitions of the region. In the north, the boundaries largely coincide because the Drava River is considered to be the northern border of Slavonia as a geographic region,[[56]](#cite_note-Kraljević-Razgraničenje-1947-57) but this excludes Baranya from the geographic region's definition even though this territory is part of a county otherwise associated with Slavonia.[[94]](#cite_note-Heritage-modern-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)[[96]](#cite_note-97) The western boundary of the geographic region is not specifically defined and it was variously defined through history depending on the political divisions of Croatia.[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27) The eastern Croatia, as a geographic term, largely overlaps most definitions of Slavonia. It is defined as the territory of the Brod-Posavina, Osijek-Baranja, Požega-Slavonia, Virovitica-Podravina and Vukovar-Syrmia counties, including Baranya.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

Slavonia is entirely located in the [Pannonian Basin](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin"), one of three major [geomorphological](/wiki/Geomorphology "Geomorphology") parts of Croatia.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The Pannonian Basin took shape through [Miocenian](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene") thinning and [subsidence](/wiki/Subsidence "Subsidence") of crust structures formed during Late [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") [Variscan orogeny](/wiki/Variscan_orogeny "Variscan orogeny"). The Paleozoic and [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") structures are visible in [Papuk](/wiki/Papuk "Papuk"), [Psunj](/wiki/Psunj "Psunj") and other Slavonian mountains. The processes also led to the formation of a [stratovolcanic](/wiki/Stratovolcano "Stratovolcano") chain in the basin 17 – 12 [Mya](/wiki/Mya_(unit) "Mya (unit)") (million years ago) and intensified subsidence observed until 5 Mya as well as [flood basalts](/wiki/Flood_basalt "Flood basalt") about 7.5 Mya. Contemporary uplift of the [Carpathian Mountains](/wiki/Carpathian_Mountains "Carpathian Mountains") prevented water flowing to the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"), and the [Pannonian Sea](/wiki/Pannonian_Sea "Pannonian Sea") formed in the basin. Sediments were transported to the basin from uplifting Carpathian and [Dinaric mountains](/wiki/Dinaric_Alps "Dinaric Alps"), with particularly deep fluvial sediments being deposited in the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") during the uplift of the [Transdanubian Mountains](/wiki/Transdanubian_Mountains "Transdanubian Mountains").[[99]](#cite_note-100) Ultimately, up to 3,000 metres (9,800 feet) of the sediment was deposited in the basin, and the Pannonian sea eventually drained through the [Iron Gate](/wiki/Iron_Gate_(Danube) "Iron Gate (Danube)") gorge.[[100]](#cite_note-101) In the southern Pannonian Basin, the [Neogene](/wiki/Neogene "Neogene") to [Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary") sediment depth is normally lower, averaging 500 to 1,500 metres (1,600 to 4,900 feet), except in central parts of depressions formed by [subduction](/wiki/Subduction "Subduction")—around 4,000 metres (13,000 feet) in the Slavonia-Syrmia depression, 5,500 metres (18,000 feet) in the Sava depression and nearly 7,000 metres (23,000 feet) in the Drava depression, with the deepest sediment found between Virovitica and Slatina.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

The results of those processes are large [plains](/wiki/Plain "Plain") in eastern Slavonia, Baranya and Syrmia, as well as in river valleys, especially along the Sava, Drava and [Kupa](/wiki/Kupa "Kupa"). The plains are interspersed by the [horst](/wiki/Horst_(geology) "Horst (geology)") and [graben](/wiki/Graben "Graben") structures, believed to have broken the Pannonian Sea surface as [islands](/wiki/Pannonian_island_mountains "Pannonian island mountains").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The tallest among such landforms in Slavonia are 984-metre (3,228 ft) [Psunj](/wiki/Psunj "Psunj"), and 953-metre (3,127 ft) Papuk—flanking the [Požega Valley](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega_Valley "Požega Valley") from the west and the north.[[92]](#cite_note-DZS-Stat2010-93) These two and [Krndija](/wiki/Krndija "Krndija"), adjacent to Papuk, consist mostly of Paleozoic rocks which are 350 – 300 million years old. [Požeška Gora](/wiki/Po%C5%BEe%C5%A1ka_Gora "Požeška Gora") and [Dilj](/wiki/Dilj "Dilj"), to the east of Psunj and enveloping the valley from the south, consist of much more recent Neogene rocks, but Požeška Gora also contains Upper [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") sediments and [igneous rocks](/wiki/Igneous_rocks "Igneous rocks") forming the main, 30-kilometre (19 mi) [ridge](/wiki/Ridge "Ridge") of the hill and representing the largest igneous landform in Croatia. A smaller igneous landform is also present on Papuk, near Voćin.[[102]](#cite_note-103) The two mountains, as well as [Moslavačka gora](/wiki/Moslava%C4%8Dka_gora "Moslavačka gora"), west of Pakrac, are possible remnants of a [volcanic arc](/wiki/Volcanic_arc "Volcanic arc") related to [Alpine orogeny](/wiki/Alpine_orogeny "Alpine orogeny")—uplifting of the Dinaric Alps.[[103]](#cite_note-EGU-104) The Đakovo – Vukovar [loess](/wiki/Loess "Loess") plain, extending eastward from Dilj and representing the watershed between the [Vuka](/wiki/Vuka_(river) "Vuka (river)") and [Bosut](/wiki/Bosut_(river) "Bosut (river)") rivers, gradually rises to the [Fruška Gora](/wiki/Fru%C5%A1ka_Gora "Fruška Gora") south of Ilok.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

#### Hydrography and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Hydrography and climate")]

Further information: [Lake Slavonia](/wiki/Lake_Slavonia "Lake Slavonia")

The largest rivers in Slavonia are found along or near its borders—the Danube, Sava and Drava. The length of the Danube, flowing along the eastern border of Slavonia and through the cities of Vukovar and Ilok, is 188 kilometres (117 miles), and its main tributaries are the Drava 112-kilometre (70 mi) and the Vuka. The Drava discharges into the Danube near [Aljmaš](/wiki/Aljma%C5%A1 "Aljmaš"), east of Osijek, while mouth of the Vuka is located in Vukovar.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/River_Drava_%2829799981721%29.jpg/250px-River_Drava_%2829799981721%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:River_Drava_(29799981721).jpg)

River [Drava](/wiki/Drava "Drava") in [Osijek](/wiki/Osijek "Osijek")

Major tributaries of the Sava, flowing along the southern border of Slavonia and through cities of Slavonski Brod and Županja are 89-kilometre (55 mi) the [Orljava](/wiki/Orljava "Orljava") flowing through Požega, and the Bosut—whose 151-kilometre (94 mi) course in Slavonia takes it through Vinkovci. There are no large lakes in Slavonia. The largest ones are Lake Kopačevo whose surface area varies between 1.5 and 3.5 square kilometres (0.58 and 1.35 square miles), and Borovik [Reservoir](/wiki/Reservoir "Reservoir") covering 2.5 square kilometres (0.97 square miles).[[92]](#cite_note-DZS-Stat2010-93) The Lake Kopačevo is connected to the Danube via Hulovski canal, situated within the [Kopački Rit](/wiki/Kopa%C4%8Dki_Rit "Kopački Rit") [wetland](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland"),[[105]](#cite_note-106) while the Lake Borovik is an artificial lake created in 1978 in the upper course of the Vuka River.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Virgrad.jpg/250px-Virgrad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgrad.jpg)

Spačva forest, the largest complete common [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") forest in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")

The entirety of Slavonia belongs to the [Danube basin](/wiki/Danube_basin "Danube basin") and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea") catchment area, but it is divided in two sub-basins. One of those drains into the Sava—itself a Danube tributary—and the other into the Drava or directly into the Danube. The [drainage divide](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") between the two sub-basins runs along the Papuk and Krndija mountains, in effect tracing the southern boundary of the Virovitica-Podravina County and the northern boundary of Požega-Slavonia County, cuts through the Osijek-Podravina County north of Đakovo, and finally bisects the Vukovar-Syrmia County running between Vukovar and Vinkovci to reach Fruška Gora southwest of Ilok. All of Brod-Posavina County is located in the Sava sub-basin.[[107]](#cite_note-Divides-Ordinance-108)

Most of Croatia, including Slavonia, has a moderately warm and rainy [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") as defined by the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"). Mean annual temperature averages 10 to 12 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (50 to 54 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")), with the warmest month, July, averaging just below 22 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (72 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")). Temperature peaks are more pronounced in the continental areas—the lowest temperature of −27.8 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (−18.0 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")) was recorded on 24 January 1963 in Slavonski Brod,[[108]](#cite_note-109) and the highest temperature of 40.5 [°C](/wiki/Celsius "Celsius") (104.9 [°F](/wiki/Fahrenheit "Fahrenheit")) was recorded on 5 July 1950 in Đakovo.[[109]](#cite_note-110) The lowest level of precipitation is recorded in the eastern parts of Slavonia at less than 700 millimetres (28 inches) per year, mostly during the [growing season](/wiki/Growing_season "Growing season"). The western parts of Slavonia receive 900 to 1,000 millimetres (35 to 39 inches) precipitation. Low winter temperatures and the distribution of precipitation throughout the year normally result in snow cover, and freezing rivers—requiring use of [icebreakers](/wiki/Icebreaker "Icebreaker"), and in extreme cases explosives,[[110]](#cite_note-111) to maintain the flow of water and navigation.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Slavonia receives more than 2,000 hours of sunshine per year on average. Prevailing winds are light to moderate, northeasterly and southwesterly.[[92]](#cite_note-DZS-Stat2010-93)

According to the 2011 census, the total population of the five counties of Slavonia was 806,192, accounting for 19% of population of Croatia. The largest portion of the total population of Slavonia lives in Osijek-Baranja county, followed by Vukovar-Syrmia county. Požega-Slavonia county is the least populous county of Slavonia. Overall the population density stands at 64.2 persons per square kilometre. The population density ranges from 77.6 to 40.9 persons per square kilometre, with the highest density recorded in Brod-Posavina county and the lowest in Virovitica-Podravina county. Osijek is the largest city in Slavonia, followed by Slavonski Brod, Vinkovci and Vukovar. Other cities in Slavonia have populations below 20,000.[[93]](#cite_note-cbs-2011-94) According to the 2001 census, [Croats](/wiki/Croats "Croats") account for 85.6 percent of population of Slavonia, and the most significant ethnic minorities are Serbs and [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians_of_Croatia "Hungarians of Croatia"), comprising 8.8 percent and 1.4 percent of the population respectively. The largest portion of the Serb minority was recorded in Vukovar-Syrmia county (15 percent), while the largest Hungarian minority, in both relative and absolute terms, was observed in Osijek-Baranja county. The census recorded 85.4% of the population declaring themselves as [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), with further 4.4% belonging to [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") and 0.7% [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"). 3.1% declared themselves as [non-religious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), [agnostics](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism") or declined to declare their religion. The most widely used language in the region is [Croatian](/wiki/Croatian_language "Croatian language"), declared as the [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language") by 93.6% of the total population, followed by [Serbian](/wiki/Serbian_language "Serbian language") (2.6%) and [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language") (1.0%).[[112]](#cite_note-Census_2001-113)

The demographic history of Slavonia is characterised by significant migrations, as is that of Croatia as a whole, starting with the arrival of the Croats, between the 6th and 9th centuries.[[113]](#cite_note-Mužić-249-293-114) Following the establishment of the personal union of Croatia and Hungary in 1102,[[16]](#cite_note-HR-HU-Heka-17) and the joining of the Habsburg monarchy in 1527,[[18]](#cite_note-Povijest-saborovanja-19) the Hungarian and German speaking population of Croatia began gradually increasing in number. The processes of [Magyarization](/wiki/Magyarization "Magyarization") and Germanization varied in intensity but persisted until the beginning of the 20th century.[[34]](#cite_note-Ante_Čuvalo_2008_13–27-35)[[114]](#cite_note-115) The Ottoman conquests initiated a westward migration of parts of the Croatian population;[[115]](#cite_note-116) the Burgenland Croats are direct descendants of some of those settlers.[[27]](#cite_note-BurgenlandCro-28) To replace the fleeing Croats the Habsburgs called on the Orthodox populations of Bosnia and Serbia to provide military service in the Croatian Military Frontier. Serb migration into this region peaked during the [Great Serb Migrations](/wiki/Great_Serb_Migrations "Great Serb Migrations") of 1690 and 1737–39.[[30]](#cite_note-Indiana_University_Press-31) Following the collapse of Austria-Hungary in 1918, the Hungarian population declined, due to emigration and ethnic bias. The changes were especially significant in the areas north of the Drava river, and Baranja County where they represented the majority before World War I.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

Since the end of the 19th century there was substantial economic emigration abroad from Croatia in general.[[117]](#cite_note-Geografija-migrations-118)[[118]](#cite_note-119) After World War I, the Yugoslav regime confiscated up to 50 percent of properties and encouraged settlement of the land by Serb volunteers and war veterans in Slavonia,[[26]](#cite_note-Blagojević-27) only to have them evicted and replaced by up to 70,000 new settlers by the regime during World War II.[[119]](#cite_note-120) During World War II and in the period immediately following the war, there were further significant demographic changes, as the German-speaking population, the [Danube Swabians](/wiki/Danube_Swabians "Danube Swabians"), were either forced or otherwise compelled to leave—reducing their number from the prewar German population of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") of 500,000, living in Slavonia and other parts of present-day Croatia and Serbia, to the figure of 62,000 recorded in the 1953 census.[[120]](#cite_note-121) The 1940s and the 1950s in Yugoslavia were marked by colonisation of settlements where the displaced Germans used to live, by people from the mountainous parts of Bosnia and Herzegovina, Serbia and Montenegro, and migrations to larger cities spurred on by the development of industry.[[121]](#cite_note-122) [*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In the 1960s and 1970s, another wave of economic migrants left—largely moving to [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[122]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Canada-123)[[123]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Australia-124)[[124]](#cite_note-MVPEI-Diaspora-125)

The most recent changes to the ethnic composition of Slavonian counties occurred between censuses conducted in 1991 and 2001. The 1991 census recorded a heterogenous population consisting mostly of Croats and Serbs—at 72 percent and 17 percent of the total population respectively. The Croatian War of Independence, and the ethnic fracturing of Yugoslavia that preceded it, caused an exodus of the Croat population followed by an exodus of Serbs. The return of refugees since the end of hostilities is not complete—a majority of Croat refugees returned, while fewer Serbs did. In addition, ethnic Croats moved to Slavonia from Bosnia and Herzegovina and from Serbia.[[84]](#cite_note-Babić-85)

## Economy and transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy and transport")]

See also: [Economy of Croatia](/wiki/Economy_of_Croatia "Economy of Croatia") and [Transport in Croatia](/wiki/Transport_in_Croatia "Transport in Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg/250px-20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg)](/wiki/File:20230429.Donau.zwischen_km_1255_und_1333.-032.1.jpg)

Tourist cruise on the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") in eastern Slavonia

The economy of Slavonia is largely based on [wholesale](/wiki/Wholesale "Wholesale") and [retail](/wiki/Retail "Retail") trade and [processing industry](/wiki/Processing_industry "Processing industry"). [Food processing](/wiki/Food_processing "Food processing") is one of the most significant types of the processing industries in the region, supporting agricultural production in the area and encompassing [meat packing](/wiki/Meat_packing "Meat packing"), [fruit and vegetable processing](/wiki/Canning "Canning"), [sugar refining](/wiki/Sugar_refining "Sugar refining"), [confectionery](/wiki/Confectionery "Confectionery") and [dairy industry](/wiki/Dairy_industry "Dairy industry"). In addition, there are [wineries](/wiki/Winery "Winery") in the region that are significant to [economy of Croatia](/wiki/Economy_of_Croatia "Economy of Croatia"). Other types of the processing industry significant to Slavonia are [wood processing](/wiki/Wood_processing "Wood processing"), including production of [furniture](/wiki/Furniture "Furniture"), [cellulose](/wiki/Cellulose "Cellulose"), paper and [cardboard](/wiki/Cardboard_(paper_product) "Cardboard (paper product)"); [metalworking](/wiki/Metalworking "Metalworking"), [textile industry](/wiki/Textile_industry "Textile industry") and [glass production](/wiki/Glass_production "Glass production"). [Transport](/wiki/Transport "Transport") and [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") are two further significant economic activities in Slavonia.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Most_Drava.jpg/500px-Most_Drava.jpg)](/wiki/File:Most_Drava.jpg)

Osijek cable-stayed [Drava Highway Bridge](/w/index.php?title=Drava_Highway_Bridge&action=edit&redlink=1 "Drava Highway Bridge (page does not exist)"), the [longest bridge](/wiki/List_of_bridges_in_Croatia "List of bridges in Croatia") in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia"). (81 metres longer than the [Pelješac bridge](/wiki/Pelje%C5%A1ac_bridge "Pelješac bridge")).

The largest industrial centre of Slavonia is Osijek, followed by other county seats—Slavonski Brod, Virovitica, Požega and Vukovar, as well as several other cities, especially Vinkovci.[[126]](#cite_note-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128)[[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130)[[130]](#cite_note-131)

The gross domestic product (GDP) of the five counties in Slavonia combined (in year 2008) amounted to 6,454 million [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), or 8,005 euro per capita—27.5% below Croatia's national average. The GDP of the five counties represented 13.6% of Croatia's GDP.[[131]](#cite_note-132) Several [Pan-European transport corridors](/wiki/Pan-European_corridors "Pan-European corridors") run through Slavonia: corridor Vc as the [A5 motorway](/wiki/A5_(Croatia) "A5 (Croatia)"), corridor X as the [A3 motorway](/wiki/A3_(Croatia) "A3 (Croatia)") and a double-track railway spanning Slavonia from west to east, and corridor VII—the Danube River waterway.[[132]](#cite_note-EU-Corridors-133) The waterway is accessed through the Port of Vukovar, the largest Croatian river port, situated on the Danube itself, and the Port of Osijek on the Drava River, 14.5 kilometres (9.0 miles) away from confluence of the rivers.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

Another major sector of the economy of Slavonia is agriculture, which also provides part of the raw materials for the processing industry. Out of 1,077,403 hectares (2,662,320 acres) of utilized agricultural land in Croatia, 493,878 hectares (1,220,400 acres), or more than 45%, are found in Slavonia, with the largest portion of the land situated in the Osijek-Baranja and Vukovar-Syrmia counties. The largest areas are used for production of [cereals](/wiki/Cereal "Cereal") and [oilseeds](/wiki/Oilseed "Oilseed"), covering 574,916 hectares (1,420,650 acres) and 89,348 hectares (220,780 acres) respectively. Slavonia's share in Croatia's agriculturally productive land is greatest in the production of cereals (53.5%), [legumes](/wiki/Legume "Legume") (46.8%), oilseeds (88.8%), sugar beet (90%), tobacco (97.9%), plants used in pharmaceutical or perfume industry (80.9%), flowers, seedlings and seeds (80.3%) and plants used in the textile industry (69%). Slavonia also contributes 25.7% of cattle, 42.7% of pigs and 20% of the poultry stock of Croatia. There are 5,138 hectares (12,700 acres) of vineyards in Slavonia, representing 18.6% of total vineyards area in Croatia. Production of fruit and nuts also takes up a significant agricultural area. Apple orchards cover 1,261 hectares (3,120 acres), representing 42.3% of Croatia's apple plantations, plums are produced in orchards encompassing 450 hectares (1,100 acres) or 59.7% of Croatia's plum plantations and hazelnut orchards cover 319 hectares (790 acres), which account for 72.4% of hazelnut plantations in Croatia. Other significant permanent crops are cherries, pears, peaches and walnuts.[[134]](#cite_note-AGR2003-135)
