In 2010, only two companies headquartered in Slavonia ranked among top 100 [Croatian companies](/wiki/List_of_companies_of_Croatia "List of companies of Croatia")—[Belje](/w/index.php?title=Belje&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belje (page does not exist)"), agricultural industry owned by [Agrokor](/wiki/Agrokor "Agrokor"),[[139]](#cite_note-140) and [Belišće](/w/index.php?title=Beli%C5%A1%C4%87e_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belišće (company) (page does not exist)"), [paper mill](/wiki/Paper_mill "Paper mill") and paper [packaging material](/wiki/Packaging_material "Packaging material") factory,[[140]](#cite_note-141) headquartered in [Darda](/wiki/Darda,_Croatia "Darda, Croatia") and [Belišće](/wiki/Beli%C5%A1%C4%87e "Belišće") respectively, both in Osijek-Baranja County. Belje ranks as the 44th and Belišće as the 99th largest Croatian company by [income](/wiki/Earnings_before_interest_and_taxes "Earnings before interest and taxes"). Other significant businesses in the county include civil engineering company [Osijek-Koteks](/w/index.php?title=Osijek-Koteks&action=edit&redlink=1 "Osijek-Koteks (page does not exist)") (rank 103),[[141]](#cite_note-142) [Saponia](/wiki/Saponia "Saponia") [detergent](/wiki/Detergent "Detergent") and [personal care](/wiki/Personal_care "Personal care") product factory (rank 138),[[142]](#cite_note-143) [Biljemerkant](/w/index.php?title=Biljemerkant&action=edit&redlink=1 "Biljemerkant (page does not exist)") retail business (rank 145),[[143]](#cite_note-144) and [Našicecement](/w/index.php?title=Na%C5%A1icecement&action=edit&redlink=1 "Našicecement (page does not exist)") [cement plant](/wiki/Cement_plant "Cement plant") (rank 165), a part of [Nexe Grupa](/w/index.php?title=Nexe_Grupa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Nexe Grupa (page does not exist)") construction product manufacturing company.[[144]](#cite_note-145) Sugar refining company [Viro](/wiki/Viro_(company) "Viro (company)"),[[145]](#cite_note-146) ranked the 101st and headquartered in Virovitica, is the largest company in Virovitica-Podravina County. Đuro Đaković Montaža d.d., a part of metal processing industry [Đuro Đaković Holding](/wiki/%C4%90uro_%C4%90akovi%C4%87_(company) "Đuro Đaković (company)") of Slavonski Brod,[[146]](#cite_note-147) ranks the 171st among the Croatian companies and it is the largest business in Brod-Posavina County. Another agricultural industry company, [Kutjevo d.d.](/w/index.php?title=Kutjevo_(company)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kutjevo (company) (page does not exist)"), headquartered in [Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo"), is the largest company in Požega-Slavonia County,[[147]](#cite_note-148) ranks the 194th in Croatia by business income. Finally, the largest company by income in Vukovar-Syrmia county is another Agrokor owned agricultural production company—[Vupik](/w/index.php?title=Vupik&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vupik (page does not exist)"), headquartered in Vukovar,[[148]](#cite_note-149) and ranking the 161st among the companies headquartered in Croatia.[[149]](#cite_note-150)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [Culture of Croatia](/wiki/Culture_of_Croatia "Culture of Croatia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/1910%2C_Miroslav_Kraljevic%2C_Autoportret_sa_psom%2C_ulje%2C_110x85%2C5%2C_Moderna_galerija_Zagreb.jpg/250px-1910%2C_Miroslav_Kraljevic%2C_Autoportret_sa_psom%2C_ulje%2C_110x85%2C5%2C_Moderna_galerija_Zagreb.jpg)](/wiki/File:1910,_Miroslav_Kraljevic,_Autoportret_sa_psom,_ulje,_110x85,5,_Moderna_galerija_Zagreb.jpg)

[Miroslav Kraljević](/wiki/Miroslav_Kraljevi%C4%87 "Miroslav Kraljević"), self-portrait

The [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage") of Slavonia represents a blend of social influences through its history, especially since the end of the 17th century, and the traditional culture. A particular impact was made by [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") art and architecture of the 18th century, when the cities of Slavonia started developing after the Ottoman wars ended and stability was restored to the area. The period saw great prominence of the nobility, who were awarded estates in Slavonia by the imperial court in return for their service during the wars. They included [Prince Eugene of Savoy](/wiki/Prince_Eugene_of_Savoy "Prince Eugene of Savoy"), the [House of Esterházy](/wiki/House_of_Esterh%C3%A1zy "House of Esterházy"), the House of [Odescalchi](/wiki/Odescalchi "Odescalchi"), [Philipp Karl von Eltz-Kempenich](/wiki/Philipp_Karl_von_Eltz-Kempenich "Philipp Karl von Eltz-Kempenich"), the House of [Prandau-Normann](/wiki/Prandau-Normann_Castle "Prandau-Normann Castle"), the [House of Pejačević](/wiki/House_of_Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87 "House of Pejačević") and the [House of Janković](/wiki/List_of_noble_families_of_Croatia "List of noble families of Croatia"). That in turn encouraged an influx of contemporary European culture to the region. Subsequent development of the cities and society saw the influence of [Neoclassicism](/wiki/Neoclassicism "Neoclassicism"), [Historicism](/wiki/Historicism_(art) "Historicism (art)") and especially of [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau").[[94]](#cite_note-Heritage-modern-95)

The heritage of the region includes numerous [landmarks](/wiki/Landmark "Landmark"), especially [manor houses](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house") built by the nobility in largely in the 18th and the 19th centuries. Those include [Prandau-Normann](/wiki/Prandau-Normann_Castle "Prandau-Normann Castle") and [Prandau-Mailath](/w/index.php?title=Prandau-Mailath_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Prandau-Mailath Castle (page does not exist)") manor houses in [Valpovo](/wiki/Valpovo "Valpovo") and [Donji Miholjac](/wiki/Donji_Miholjac "Donji Miholjac") respectively,[[150]](#cite_note-151)[[151]](#cite_note-152) manor houses in Baranja—in [Bilje](/wiki/Bilje,_Croatia "Bilje, Croatia"),[[152]](#cite_note-153) at a former Esterházy estate in Darda,[[153]](#cite_note-154) in [Tikveš](/wiki/Tikve%C5%A1,_Croatia "Tikveš, Croatia"),[[154]](#cite_note-155) and in [Kneževo](/wiki/Kne%C5%BEevo,_Croatia "Kneževo, Croatia").[[155]](#cite_note-156) Pejačevićs built several residences, the most representative ones among them being [manor house in Virovitica](/wiki/Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87_Castle_in_Virovitica "Pejačević Castle in Virovitica") and the [Pejačević manor house in Našice](/w/index.php?title=Peja%C4%8Devi%C4%87_manor_house_in_Na%C5%A1ice&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pejačević manor house in Našice (page does not exist)").[[156]](#cite_note-Tportal-Landmarks-157) Further east, along the Danube, there are [Odescalchi manor house](/wiki/Ilok_Castle "Ilok Castle") in Ilok,[[157]](#cite_note-158) and [Eltz manor house](/wiki/Eltz_Manor "Eltz Manor") in Vukovar—the latter sustained extensive damage during the Battle of Vukovar in 1991,[[158]](#cite_note-159) but it was reconstructed by 2011.[[159]](#cite_note-160) In the southeast of the region, the most prominent are [Kutjevo Jesuit manor house](/w/index.php?title=Kutjevo_Jesuit_manor_house&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kutjevo Jesuit manor house (page does not exist)"),[[160]](#cite_note-161) and [Cernik manor house](/w/index.php?title=Cernik_manor_house&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cernik manor house (page does not exist)"), located in [Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo") and [Cernik](/wiki/Cernik,_Brod-Posavina_County "Cernik, Brod-Posavina County") respectively.[[161]](#cite_note-162) The period also saw construction of [Tvrđa](/wiki/Tvr%C4%91a "Tvrđa") and [Brod](/wiki/Brod_Fortress "Brod Fortress") [fortifications](/wiki/Fortifications "Fortifications") in Osijek and Slavonski Brod.[[162]](#cite_note-163)[[163]](#cite_note-164) Older, medieval fortifications are preserved only as [ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins")—the largest among those being [Ružica Castle](/w/index.php?title=Ru%C5%BEica_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ružica Castle (page does not exist)") near [Orahovica](/wiki/Orahovica "Orahovica").[[164]](#cite_note-165) Another landmark dating to the 19th century is the [Đakovo Cathedral](/wiki/%C4%90akovo_Cathedral "Đakovo Cathedral")—hailed by the [Pope John XXIII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXIII "Pope John XXIII") as the most beautiful church situated between [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice") and [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul").[[165]](#cite_note-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Erdut.jpg/250px-Erdut.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erdut.jpg)

[Erdut Castle](/wiki/Erdut_Castle "Erdut Castle"), 15th-century fortification near [Erdut](/wiki/Erdut "Erdut")[[167]](#cite_note-168)

Slavonia significantly contributed to the culture of Croatia as a whole, both through works of artists and through patrons of the arts—most notable among them being [Josip Juraj Strossmayer](/wiki/Josip_Juraj_Strossmayer "Josip Juraj Strossmayer").[[168]](#cite_note-Writers-169) Strossmayer was instrumental in the establishment of the [Yugoslav Academy of Sciences and Arts](/wiki/Croatian_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Arts "Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts"), later renamed the Croatian Academy of Sciences and Arts,[[169]](#cite_note-170) and the reestablishment of the [University of Zagreb](/wiki/University_of_Zagreb "University of Zagreb").[[170]](#cite_note-171) A number of Slavonia's artists, especially writers, made considerable contributions to Croatian culture. Nineteenth-century writers who are most significant in Croatian literature include [Josip Eugen Tomić](/wiki/Josip_Eugen_Tomi%C4%87 "Josip Eugen Tomić"), [Josip Kozarac](/wiki/Josip_Kozarac "Josip Kozarac"), and [Miroslav Kraljević](/w/index.php?title=Miroslav_Kraljevi%C4%87_(writer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miroslav Kraljević (writer) (page does not exist)")—author of the first Croatian novel.[[168]](#cite_note-Writers-169) Significant twentieth-century poets and writers in Slavonia were [Dobriša Cesarić](/wiki/Dobri%C5%A1a_Cesari%C4%87 "Dobriša Cesarić"), [Dragutin Tadijanović](/wiki/Dragutin_Tadijanovi%C4%87 "Dragutin Tadijanović"), [Ivana Brlić-Mažuranić](/wiki/Ivana_Brli%C4%87-Ma%C5%BEurani%C4%87 "Ivana Brlić-Mažuranić") and [Antun Gustav Matoš](/wiki/Antun_Gustav_Mato%C5%A1 "Antun Gustav Matoš").[[171]](#cite_note-172) Painters associated with Slavonia, who contributed greatly to Croatian art, were [Miroslav Kraljević](/wiki/Miroslav_Kraljevi%C4%87 "Miroslav Kraljević") and [Bela Čikoš Sesija](/wiki/Bela_%C4%8Ciko%C5%A1_Sesija "Bela Čikoš Sesija").[[172]](#cite_note-173)

Slavonia is a distinct region of Croatia in terms of ethnological factors in traditional music. It is a region where traditional culture is preserved through [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") festivals. Typical traditional music instruments belong to the [tamburica](/wiki/Tamburica "Tamburica") and [bagpipe](/wiki/Croatian_bagpipes "Croatian bagpipes") family.[[173]](#cite_note-174) The tamburica is the most representative musical instrument associated with Slavonia's traditional culture. It developed from music instruments brought by the Ottomans during their rule of Slavonia, becoming an integral part of the traditional music, its use surpassing or even replacing the use of bagpipes and [gusle](/wiki/Gusle "Gusle").[[174]](#cite_note-175) A distinct form of traditional song, originating in Slavonia, the [bećarac](/wiki/Be%C4%87arac "Bećarac"), is recognized as an [intangible cultural heritage](/wiki/UNESCO_Intangible_Cultural_Heritage_Lists "UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO").[[175]](#cite_note-176)[[176]](#cite_note-177)

Out of 122 Croatia's universities and other institutions of [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education"),[[177]](#cite_note-178) Slavonia is home to one university—[Josip Juraj Strossmayer University of Osijek](/wiki/Josip_Juraj_Strossmayer_University_of_Osijek "Josip Juraj Strossmayer University of Osijek")—[[178]](#cite_note-179) as well as three [polytechnics](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology") in Požega, Slavonski Brod and Vukovar, as well as a [college](/wiki/College "College") in Virovitica—all set up and run by the [government](/wiki/Ministry_of_Science,_Education_and_Sports_(Croatia) "Ministry of Science, Education and Sports (Croatia)").[[179]](#cite_note-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181) The University of Osijek, has been established in 1975,[[181]](#cite_note-182) but the first institution of higher education in the city was *Studium Philosophicum Essekini* founded in 1707, and active until 1780.[[182]](#cite_note-183) Another historical institution of higher education was *Academia Posegana* operating in Požega between 1761 and 1776,[[183]](#cite_note-184) as an extension of a [gymnasium](/wiki/Gymnasium_(school) "Gymnasium (school)") operating in the city continuously,[[184]](#cite_note-185) since it opened in 1699 as the first [secondary education](/wiki/Secondary_education "Secondary education") school in Slavonia.[[185]](#cite_note-186)

### Cuisine and wines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Slavonia&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cuisine and wines")]

See also: [Cuisine of Croatia](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Croatia "Cuisine of Croatia") and [Croatian wine](/wiki/Croatian_wine "Croatian wine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Gra%C5%A1evina_i_traminac.jpg/250px-Gra%C5%A1evina_i_traminac.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gra%C5%A1evina_i_traminac.jpg)

Graševina wine ([Welshriesling](/wiki/Welshriesling "Welshriesling")) and [Gewürztraminer](/wiki/Gew%C3%BCrztraminer "Gewürztraminer") (Traminac) wine, dry white wine, produced in [Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo"), [Požega-Slavonia County](/wiki/Po%C5%BEega-Slavonia_County "Požega-Slavonia County").

The cuisine of Slavonia reflects cultural influences on the region through the diversity of its culinary influences. The most significant among those were from [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_cuisine "Hungarian cuisine"), [Viennese](/wiki/Viennese_cuisine "Viennese cuisine"), Central European, as well as [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_cuisine "Turkish cuisine") and [Arab cuisines](/wiki/Arab_cuisine "Arab cuisine") brought by series of conquests and accompanying social influences. The ingredients of traditional dishes are [pickled](/wiki/Pickling "Pickling") vegetables, dairy products and [smoked meats](/wiki/Smoked_meat "Smoked meat").[[186]](#cite_note-187) The most famous traditional preserved meat product is [kulen](/wiki/Kulen "Kulen"), one of a handful Croatian products protected by the EU as indigenous products.[[187]](#cite_note-188)Among the best-known cooked dishes of the region is [Slavonian čobanac](/wiki/Slavonian_%C4%8Dobanac "Slavonian čobanac"), a traditional meat [stew](/wiki/Stew "Stew") widely regarded as one of the most characteristic dishes of Slavonian cuisine.[[188]](#cite_note-189)

Slavonia is one of Croatia's winemaking sub-regions, a part of its continental winegrowing region. The best known winegrowing areas of Slavonia are centered on [Đakovo](/wiki/%C4%90akovo "Đakovo"), [Ilok](/wiki/Ilok "Ilok") and [Kutjevo](/wiki/Kutjevo "Kutjevo"), where [Graševina](/wiki/Gra%C5%A1evina "Graševina") grapes are predominant, but other [cultivars](/wiki/Cultivar "Cultivar") are increasingly present.[[189]](#cite_note-190) In past decades, an increasing quantity of wine production in Slavonia was accompanied by increasing quality and growing recognition at home and abroad.[[190]](#cite_note-191) Grape vines were first grown in the region of Ilok, as early as the 3rd century AD. The oldest Slavonian wine cellar still in continuous use for winemaking is located in Kutjevo—built in 1232 by [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians").[[191]](#cite_note-192)

Slavonian [oak](/wiki/Oak_(wine) "Oak (wine)") is used to make *botti*, large barrels traditionally used in the [Piedmont region](/wiki/Piemonte_(wine) "Piemonte (wine)") of [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to make [nebbiolo](/wiki/Nebbiolo "Nebbiolo") wines.[[192]](#cite_note-193)
